mission.title: Install and Configure MetalLB (Bare-metal Load Balancer)
mission.description: MetalLB is a load-balancer implementation for bare metal Kubernetes clusters, utilizing standard routing protocols. It is particularly useful in environments where cloud-native load balancers are not available. Key trade-offs include the need for manual configuration of IP address ranges and potential complexity in network setup.

steps.title: Add the MetalLB Helm repository
steps.description:
This step adds the official MetalLB Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration.

```bash

helm repo add metallb https://metallb.universe.tf

helm repo update

```

> This command adds the MetalLB repository.
> Updates your local Helm chart repository cache.

steps.title: Install MetalLB Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs)
steps.description:
This step installs the necessary CRDs for MetalLB to function properly.

```bash

kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/metallb/metallb/v0.15.3/manifests/namespace.yaml

kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/metallb/metallb/v0.15.3/manifests/metallb.yaml

```

> Creates the MetalLB namespace.
> Installs the MetalLB components.

steps.title: Install MetalLB using Helm
steps.description:
This step installs the MetalLB operator using Helm, specifying the namespace and version.

```bash

helm install metallb metallb/metallb --namespace metallb-system --create-namespace --version 0.15.3

```

> Installs MetalLB in the metallb-system namespace.

steps.title: Create a MetalLB ConfigMap
steps.description:
This step creates a ConfigMap to configure MetalLB with a pool of IP addresses for load balancing.

```bash

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  namespace: metallb-system
  name: config
data:
  config: |
    address-pools:
    - name: default
      protocol: layer2
      addresses:
      - 192.168.1.200-192.168.1.250
EOF

```

> Replace the IP range with your own network's range.
commands:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  namespace: metallb-system
  name: config
data:
  config: |
    address-pools:
    - name: default
      protocol: layer2
      addresses:
      - 192.168.1.200-192.168.1.250
EOF

steps.title: Verify MetalLB installation
steps.description:
This step checks if the MetalLB pods are running and ready.

```bash

kubectl get pods -n metallb-system

```

> You should see MetalLB pods in a Running state.

uninstall.title: Uninstall MetalLB
uninstall.description:
This step removes MetalLB and its associated resources. Be cautious as this may lead to data loss if services depend on MetalLB.

```bash

helm uninstall metallb --namespace metallb-system

kubectl delete namespace metallb-system

```

> Uninstalls the MetalLB Helm release.
> Deletes the MetalLB namespace and all resources within it.

upgrade.title: Upgrade MetalLB
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you back up your configurations. This step upgrades MetalLB to the latest version.

```bash

kubectl get configmap config -n metallb-system -o yaml > metallb-config-backup.yaml

helm upgrade metallb metallb/metallb --namespace metallb-system --version 0.15.3

kubectl get pods -n metallb-system

```

> Backup the current MetalLB configuration.
> Upgrades MetalLB to the specified version.
> Verify that the MetalLB pods are running after the upgrade.
commands: kubectl get configmap config -n metallb-system -o yaml > metallb-config-backup.yaml

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to allocate IP address'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may occur if the specified IP range is exhausted or incorrectly configured.

**Fix:**
Check the ConfigMap for correct IP range configuration and ensure there are available IPs.

troubleshooting.title: MetalLB pods are in 'CrashLoopBackOff' state
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This can happen due to misconfiguration in the ConfigMap or missing dependencies.

**Fix:**
Review the logs of the MetalLB pods using 'kubectl logs <pod-name> -n metallb-system' to identify the issue.

troubleshooting.title: Load balancer service is not getting an external IP
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may be due to MetalLB not being able to communicate with the network properly.

**Fix:**
Ensure that MetalLB is correctly configured and that your network allows for the specified IP range.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'No available IP addresses'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that the IP address pool is either empty or not configured correctly.

**Fix:**
Check the ConfigMap for the address pool configuration and ensure the addresses are within the correct subnet.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for BGP peering, improved layer 2 mode stability.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: No specific migration steps required when upgrading from v0.14.

resolution.summary: You should see the MetalLB pods running with the command 'kubectl get pods -n metallb-system' showing all pods in the Running state.

EOF

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running version 1.25 or higher and the necessary tools installed.
